subject: ap-calculus-ab
title: Two Particles on x-axis — Velocity, Opposite Directions & Position
stimulus: Two particles, $H$ and $J$, are moving along the $x$-axis. For $0 \le t \le 5$, the position of particle $H$ at time $t$ is given by $x_H(t) = e^{t^2 - 4t}$ and the velocity of particle $J$ at time $t$ is given by $v_J(t) = 2t(t^2-1)^3$.

parts.prompt: Find the velocity of particle $H$ at time $t = 1$. Show the work that leads to your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: P1 earned for $x_H'(t)$, $x_H'(1)$, or $(2t-4)e^{t^2-4t}$ or $(2\cdot1-4)e^{1-4}$. P2 earned for $-2e^{-3}$ or equivalent. An unsupported answer of $-2e^{-3}$ earns P2 but not P1.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: presents $x_H'(t)$, $x_H'(1)$, or $(2t-4)e^{t^2-4t}$ (chain rule applied to $x_H$).

sample_responses.commentary: Differentiates $e^{t^2-4t}$ using the chain rule: derivative of exponent is $2t-4$.
An unsupported answer earns P2 but not P1; must show the differentiation work.
At $t=1$: $2t-4 = 2-4 = -2$, not $+2$; the velocity is negative at $t=1$.

parts.prompt: During what open intervals of time $t$, for $0 < t < 5$, are particles $H$ and $J$ moving in opposite directions? Give a reason for your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Particle H: $v_H < 0$ for $0 < t < 2$ (moving left), $v_H > 0$ for $2 < t < 5$ (moving right). Particle J: $v_J < 0$ for $0 < t < 1$ (moving left), $v_J > 0$ for $1 < t < 5$ (moving right). Opposite directions only when one is positive and the other negative: $1 < t < 2$. Must analyze both particles for P5.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: sets $v_H=0$ or $v_J=0$, or identifies $t=2$ for particle H or $t=1$ for particle J in $(0,5)$.

sample_responses.commentary: Identifies the zero of $v_H$ at $t=2$ and the relevant zero of $v_J$ at $t=1$ in the open interval.
Identifying the correct interval directly is one of the ways to earn P3.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: complete sign analysis of velocity for at least one particle on $(0,5)$.

sample_responses.response: For particle $H$: $v_H < 0$ for $0<t<2$ (moving left), $v_H>0$ for $2<t<5$ (moving right).
sample_responses.commentary: Complete sign analysis of $v_H$ on the full interval; this alone earns P4.

sample_responses.response: $v_H$ changes sign at $t=2$ and $v_J$ changes at $t=1$ (no full direction analysis for either particle).
sample_responses.commentary: Must provide a sign analysis on the full interval for at least one particle, not just identify the zero.

scoring_points.description: Correct answer with complete reasoning: particles move in opposite directions for $1 < t < 2$ (H moves left, J moves right)

required_elements: Correct answer with complete reasoning: particles move in opposite directions for $1 < t < 2$ (H moves left, J moves right)

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: correct answer $1<t<2$ with correct full direction analyses for both particles.

sample_responses.response: Particle $H$: left for $0<t<2$, right for $2<t<5$. Particle $J$: left for $0<t<1$, right for $1<t<5$. Opposite directions: $1<t<2$ (H left, J right).
sample_responses.commentary: Provides complete analyses of both particles and correctly intersects the intervals where they move in opposite directions.

sample_responses.response: Particles move in opposite directions for $0<t<2$.
sample_responses.commentary: On $0<t<1$ both particles move left (same direction); the opposite-direction interval is only $1<t<2$.

parts.prompt: It can be shown that $v_J'(2) > 0$. Is the speed of particle $J$ increasing, decreasing, or neither at time $t = 2$? Give a reason for your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Since $v_J(2) = 108 > 0$ and $v_J'(2) > 0$ (same sign), speed is increasing. Can import sign of $v_J(2)$ from part (b). An evaluation of $v_J(2)$ is not required; stating $v_J(2) > 0$ (consistent with part b analysis) suffices.

scoring_points.description: Speed is increasing at $t = 2$ because $v_J(2) = 2(2)(3)^3 = 108 > 0$ and $v_J'(2) > 0$ (given), so velocity and acceleration have the same sign
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: speed is increasing because $v_J(2)>0$ and $v_J'(2)>0$ have the same sign.

sample_responses.response: From part (b), $v_J(t)>0$ for $1<t<5$, so $v_J(2)>0$. Given $v_J'(2)>0$. Since $v_J$ and $v_J'$ have the same sign at $t=2$, the speed is increasing.
sample_responses.commentary: Imports the sign of $v_J(2)$ from part (b) and combines with the given $v_J'(2)>0$ to conclude speed is increasing.

sample_responses.response: Speed is increasing because $v_J'(2)>0$ means $v_J$ is increasing.
sample_responses.commentary: Incomplete: must also establish $v_J(2)>0$; speed $=|v_J|$ increases when $v_J$ and $v_J'$ have the same sign, which requires knowing the sign of both.

parts.prompt: Particle $J$ is at position $x = 7$ at time $t = 0$. Find the position of particle $J$ at time $t = 2$. Show the work that leads to your answer.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: indefinite or definite integral with integrand $v_J(t)$ or $2t(t^2-1)^3$.

sample_responses.commentary: Accumulation form: new position = initial position + integral of velocity.
Earns P7 for the correct integrand; P8 and P9 will handle initial condition and final value.
U-substitution correctly identifies $u=t^2-1$ and evaluates the resulting power.
Dividing by $2t$ is incorrect; the $2t$ is the $du$ factor, not something to divide out separately.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: correct answer $x_J(2) = 27$ (only eligible with correct antiderivative).

sample_responses.commentary: Evaluates the definite integral using FTC and adds initial position $7$.
Must subtract $F(0)$: $\frac{(-1)^4}{4} = \frac{1}{4}$; the definite integral is $\frac{81}{4}-\frac{1}{4}=20$, not $\frac{81}{4}$.
